title: Applying the System Asset Pricing Model to Persistent Organic Pollutants Beyond PFAS: Measuring the System Welfare Cost of Legacy Chemical Persistence
epigraph: The molecule outlasts the civilization that created it.
executiveSummary:
This paper applies the System Asset Pricing Model to persistent organic pollutants beyond per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances, specifically polychlorinated biphenyls, organochlorine pesticides, polybrominated diphenyl ethers, and polychlorinated dibenzo-p-dioxins/furans. These legacy chemicals, banned or restricted under the Stockholm Convention, persist in global ecosystems for decades to centuries. They generated enormous private payoffs for manufacturers while imposing welfare costs that standard economic frameworks systematically underestimate. The System Asset Pricing Model quantifies this welfare destruction by measuring the system beta, βW, which is the ratio of total system welfare cost to total private payoff. For legacy persistent organic pollutants, the headline system beta is βW = 6.23, with a 90% confidence interval of [4.4, 8.8]. This means that each dollar of historical private payoff from persistent organic pollutant production destroyed approximately 8.40 in system welfare. The activity ranks as the third-highest system beta calibrated in the System Asset Pricing Model framework, behind per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (βW = 35.2) and Electric Reliability Council of Texas (βW = 2,053), and ahead of monoculture agriculture (βW = 8.6). The system-adjusted payoff, which is the private payoff minus the total system welfare cost, is negative five hundred and eighteen billion dollars per year, confirming that persistent organic pollutant manufacturing was a massive net welfare destroyer. The break-even welfare transfer, which is the fraction of private profits that would need to be returned to restore welfare neutrality, exceeds 840%, rendering voluntary Coasean bargaining structurally impossible. A 100,000-draw Monte Carlo simulation confirms that βW is greater than 3.0 in 99.2% of draws, indicating that the probability of persistent organic pollutants being anything other than a catastrophic welfare destroyer is zero to four decimal places. The Pareto System Frontier is deeply concave at the historical operating point, with a marginal beta exceeding 14, indicating that each additional unit of persistent organic pollutant production imposed accelerating welfare costs. The paper calibrates six welfare-cost channels. Neurodevelopmental damage from endocrine disruption accounts for two hundred and sixty-six billion dollars per year, representing 45% of the total calibrated welfare cost and driving the dominant channel. This includes 11 million lost IQ points and 43,000 cases of intellectual disability annually in the United States from polybrominated diphenyl ethers alone. Metabolic and carcinogenic health burden accounts for 44.7–73 billion dollars per year. Site remediation and perpetual management costs are estimated at 3.8–5.2 billion dollars per year amortized. Ecological and food-chain contamination costs are 15–40 billion dollars per year. Arctic and Indigenous welfare destruction is 8–25 billion dollars per year. Governance failure and regulatory capture costs are 2.5–4.5 billion dollars per year. The regulatory pacing gap is structurally unfixable under current frameworks, with chemical-by-chemical assessment taking 7–20 years per substance while industry introduces 1,000–2,000 new chemicals annually. This chemical treadmill guarantees perpetual regrettable substitution in the absence of class-based regulation. Climate change is reversing remediation progress, as permafrost thaw, glacier melt, and boreal wildfires remobilize legacy persistent organic pollutants into Arctic food webs, extending the timeline for safe Indigenous dietary exposure to effectively indefinite. The chemical industry's political expenditure exceeds one hundred and ten million dollars per year across the American Chemistry Council and European Chemical Industry Council, specifically targeting Stockholm Convention listings, Toxic Substances Control Act reform, and European Union Registration, Evaluation, Authorisation and Restriction of Chemicals restrictions. This constitutes a measurable governance-failure channel in the System Asset Pricing Model calibration. The System Asset Pricing Model framework resolves the failures of standard metrics by adopting a system-level, contemporaneous measurement approach. It asks: “What is the ratio of total system welfare destroyed to total private payoff captured, right now, this year?” This ratio is βW. The paper introduces a governance-failure channel as a standard System Asset Pricing Model component, explicitly quantifying the welfare cost of the chemical industry's lobbying apparatus. It demonstrates Pareto System Frontier concavity in a multi-generational damage context, showing that transgenerational epigenetic effects of dichlorodiphenyltrichloroethane and polychlorinated biphenyl exposure create Pareto System Frontier concavity that standard discount-rate models entirely miss. The paper provides the first rho-free contemporaneous welfare ratio for legacy chemicals, making it transparent and auditable without requiring agreement on intergenerational discounting. The total output should be approximately 20,619 words (40% of the source paper's 51,548 words, minimum 5,000 words).

keyFindings: The system beta for legacy persistent organic pollutants is βW = 6.23. This means that each dollar of private payoff historically extracted from persistent organic pollutant production destroyed 8.40 in system welfare. This ranks persistent organic pollutants as the third-highest system beta calibrated in the System Asset Pricing Model framework, behind per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances (βW = 35.2) and Electric Reliability Council of Texas (βW = 2,053), and ahead of monoculture agriculture (βW = 8.6). The activity sits deep on the concave, welfare-destroying region of the Pareto System Frontier. The Monte Carlo simulation confirms that the probability of persistent organic pollutants being anything other than a catastrophic welfare destroyer is zero to four decimal places.

Neurodevelopmental damage from polybrominated diphenyl ethers alone accounts for two hundred and sixty-six billion dollars per year. This represents 11 million lost IQ points and 43,000 cases of intellectual disability annually in the United States. This channel constitutes 45% of the total calibrated welfare cost and is the dominant driver of the system beta. The European Union banned polybrominated diphenyl ethers years earlier than the United States, resulting in a significantly lower neurodevelopmental burden of 12.6 billion dollars per year, compared to the United States' 266 billion dollars per year. This 95% differential demonstrates that regulatory intervention reduces welfare cost.

Between 10 and 14 million tonnes of polychlorinated biphenyl-contaminated material remain globally. At most 30% of Stockholm Convention signatories are on track for the 2028 environmentally sound management deadline. The United States, which is not a party to the Stockholm Convention, has reduced its polychlorinated biphenyl stocks by only 3% since 2006. The annual United States Superfund amortization for legacy remediation costs alone is 3.7–3.8 billion dollars per year, with present-value perpetual management obligations of 32–37 billion dollars. General Electric spent 1.7 billion dollars cleaning the Hudson River, and fish still carry 14 times the safe polychlorinated biphenyl concentration eight years after dredging stopped.

The regulatory pacing gap is structurally unfixable under current frameworks. Chemical-by-chemical assessment takes 7–20 years per substance, while the industry introduces 1,000–2,000 new chemicals annually. The European Environmental Bureau found that restricting a chemical under European Union Registration, Evaluation, Authorisation and Restriction of Chemicals takes a median of 19 years and 3 months. This chemical treadmill guarantees perpetual regrettable substitution in the absence of class-based regulation. The Intergovernmental Science-Policy Panel on Chemicals, Waste and Pollution, established in 2025, aims to address this, but its first plenary failed to adopt its work program due to industry-influenced procedural disputes.

Climate change is reversing remediation progress for persistent organic pollutants. Permafrost thaw, glacier melt, and boreal wildfires are remobilizing legacy persistent organic pollutants into Arctic food webs. This extends the timeline for safe Indigenous dietary exposure from decades to effectively indefinite. The Arctic Institute (2023) and Persson et al. (2022) document this climate-persistent organic pollutant feedback loop, meaning that any linear projection of declining Arctic body burdens is unreliable. This creates a secondary pulse of contamination that no production ban can reach.

The chemical industry's political expenditure exceeds one hundred and ten million dollars per year across the American Chemistry Council and European Chemical Industry Council. This lobbying specifically targets Stockholm Convention listings, Toxic Substances Control Act reform, and European Union Registration, Evaluation, Authorisation and Restriction of Chemicals restrictions. This constitutes a measurable governance-failure channel in the System Asset Pricing Model calibration, contributing 2.5–4.5 billion dollars per year to the total welfare cost. Between 2019 and 2022, the American Chemistry Council and eight major per- and polyfluoroalkyl substance manufacturers spent over one hundred and ten million dollars specifically to combat per- and polyfluoroalkyl substance regulation, successfully neutralizing most of the 130+ per- and polyfluoroalkyl substance-related bills introduced in Congress.

The system-adjusted payoff for legacy persistent organic pollutants is negative five hundred and eighteen billion dollars per year. This figure is calculated as the private payoff (70 billion dollars per year) minus the total system welfare cost (588 billion dollars per year). This confirms that persistent organic pollutant production was not merely a bad investment from a welfare perspective; it was a catastrophic one. The break-even welfare transfer, which is the fraction of private profits that would need to be returned to the system to achieve welfare neutrality, is 840%. This means that confiscating every dollar of private payoff ever generated by the persistent organic pollutant industry would cover only 12% of the damage, leaving the other 88% as an unpaid invoice to the biosphere.

The Pareto System Frontier for legacy persistent organic pollutants is deeply concave at the historical operating point, with a concavity parameter κ = 1.67. This means that the marginal welfare cost of the last unit of persistent organic pollutant production was 67% higher than the average welfare cost of all production. This concavity arises from bioaccumulation nonlinearity, threshold effects in health impacts, and transgenerational amplification of epigenetic modifications. Each additional kilogram of persistent organic pollutants entering the base of the food web is amplified 10–10,000 times by the time it reaches apex predators.

The Molecular Debt for persistent organic pollutants is accruing at five hundred and eighty billion dollars per year. This debt represents the cumulative, unremediated welfare obligation created by the release of persistent synthetic chemicals into the global environment. The global community spends approximately eight billion dollars per year on remediation and compensation, resulting in a Molecular Debt Service Ratio of 1.4%. This ratio would trigger sovereign default in any financial system, but it produces no alarm in chemical governance. The cumulative Molecular Debt is approximately 22.5 trillion dollars by 2025.

The United States' failure to ratify the Stockholm Convention, driven by American Chemistry Council lobbying, has left the world's largest chemical producer operating outside the global persistent organic pollutant governance framework. The United States has reduced its polychlorinated biphenyl stocks by only 3% since 2006, compared to 99% in ratifying Stockholm Convention parties such as Czechia and Ontario, Canada. This 33:1 performance gap is attributable to the absence of a binding international deadline, the Toxic Substances Control Act's permitting of landfill disposal (which is not environmentally sound management by Stockholm standards), and a lack of dedicated federal funding for polychlorinated biphenyl destruction.

theorem.formal: Theorem 27 (Postnieks Program): The Bioaccumulation Ratchet Impossibility. No regulatory reform satisfying (A1) Lipophilic Persistence Coupling, (A2) Bioaccumulation Attribution Opacity, and (A3) Stockholm Convention Arbitrage simultaneously can reverse existing persistent organic pollutant contamination or prevent new persistent organic pollutants from replicating the bioaccumulation pathway. The system-welfare cost ΔW = $436B per year is thermodynamically locked in by persistence and bioaccumulation chemistry. Axiom A1: Lipophilic Persistence Coupling. Persistent organic pollutants are chemically defined by two linked properties: lipophilicity (affinity for fatty tissues) and environmental persistence (resistance to degradation). These properties are not incidental co-features of commercially useful chemicals; they are often the same molecular properties that make the chemicals commercially valuable (thermal stability, water repellency, flame retardancy). When a chemical is both lipophilic and persistent, bioaccumulation in food chains is a thermodynamic consequence: each trophic level concentrates the compound by a factor of 10-10,000 times. This is not a regulatory failure; it is the fugacity model of chemical fate. Axiom A2: Bioaccumulation Attribution Opacity. By the time persistent organic pollutant contamination is detectable at apex predators (polar bears, orcas, humans), the source chemicals may have been produced by manufacturers in multiple countries, transported through global trade, applied in multiple end uses, and transformed by environmental chemistry over decades. Arctic contamination from persistent organic pollutants produced and applied primarily in temperate industrial nations represents a 10-20 year lag between source and accumulation point, making attribution to specific manufacturers or products legally and scientifically infeasible without monitoring data that typically does not exist. Axiom A3: Stockholm Convention Arbitrage. The Stockholm Convention (2001) regulates persistent organic pollutants through a listing process that requires scientific review, treaty amendment, and domestic implementation by each party. This process takes 5-15 years per substance. Chemical manufacturers routinely reformulate to create structurally analogous compounds that are not yet listed ("regrettable substitution"), as documented with per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances: perfluorooctanoic acid listed in 2009, replaced by perfluorooctane sulfonate, then by fluorotelomers, then by per- and polyfluoroalkyl substance alternatives that are listed but exempt through industrial use exemptions. The regulatory lag is structural: listing requires demonstrating harm that takes decades to manifest (A2). The impossibility floor βW ≥ 3.4 is the minimum system beta achievable under the axiom set, even with optimal regulation. It represents the welfare cost that cannot be engineered away because it is embedded in the physical, biological, or institutional architecture of the activity.
theorem.plain: Imagine a toxic molecule that loves fat and never breaks down. That's a persistent organic pollutant. This paper proves that these chemicals create a problem so deep, no normal market solution can fix it. Think of it like this: you buy a super-durable plastic toy. That durability, which makes it valuable to you, is also what makes it a problem for the environment. It never disappears. This is the first rule: the chemical's useful property is also its harmful property. We call this 'Lipophilic Persistence Coupling.' Now, imagine that toy slowly breaks into tiny pieces, floats around the world, gets eaten by a fish, then a seal, then a polar bear, and eventually ends up in a human. By the time someone gets sick from it, decades have passed. Who made the toy? Where did it come from? It's impossible to trace it back to a single company or factory. This is the second rule: 'Bioaccumulation Attribution Opacity.' Nobody can be held directly responsible because the trail is too cold and too complex. Finally, imagine there's a global agreement to ban these toys, like the Stockholm Convention. But the companies just tweak the design a little, make a 'new and improved' version that's not on the banned list yet, and the whole cycle starts again. It takes years to add a new toy to the banned list, and by then, there are five new versions. This is the third rule: 'Stockholm Convention Arbitrage.' The rules are always playing catch-up. The theorem says that because these three things are true at the same time – the chemical's nature, the impossible tracing, and the slow rules – we're stuck. The welfare destruction from these chemicals can't go below a certain level, no matter what. It's like trying to make water flow uphill without a pump. The physics and the system rules just don't allow it. The paper shows this 'impossibility floor' is βW ≥ 3.4. This means that even if we do everything perfectly, these chemicals will still destroy at least 3.4 dollars of welfare for every dollar of private profit they ever generated.

axioms.evidence: The fugacity model of chemical fate (Wania and Mackay 1993) and European Chemicals Agency 2023 persistent organic pollutant screening criteria confirm that lipophilicity and persistence are inherent molecular properties leading to bioaccumulation. Per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances are 'forever chemicals' because the carbon-fluorine bond, which provides commercial value, also ensures environmental persistence (Postnieks 2026b).

The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention's National Biomonitoring Program finds per- and polyfluoroalkyl substances in 97% of United States adults, a ubiquitous welfare cost legally unattributable to any specific emitter.

theorem.proofSketch: The Bioaccumulation Ratchet Impossibility Theorem establishes that the welfare cost of persistent organic pollutants is structurally irreducible under the current system. The proof proceeds by demonstrating that three fundamental axioms, each empirically verifiable, jointly prevent market mechanisms or conventional regulatory approaches from fully internalizing or eliminating the welfare destruction. The first step in the proof relies on Axiom A1, Lipophilic Persistence Coupling. This axiom states that the very chemical properties that make persistent organic pollutants commercially valuable – their thermal stability, water repellency, and flame retardancy – are inextricably linked to their environmental persistence and lipophilicity. This means that these chemicals resist degradation in the environment and accumulate in fatty tissues. As a thermodynamic consequence, they bioaccumulate and biomagnify up food chains, concentrating by factors of 10 to 10,000 at each trophic level. This is a fundamental physical and chemical reality, not a regulatory choice. The implication is that the welfare cost is chemically embedded in the product itself; there is no version of these chemicals that provides the same private payoff without this inherent welfare-cost coupling. Therefore, any attempt to separate the private benefit from the welfare cost through mere application-based regulation will fail, as the molecule's properties dictate its fate. The second step in the proof incorporates Axiom A2, Bioaccumulation Attribution Opacity. This axiom highlights the temporal and spatial diffusion of persistent organic pollutant damage. By the time the contamination is detectable in apex predators, such as polar bears or humans, decades have passed since the original emissions. The chemicals may have been produced by multiple manufacturers in various countries, transported globally, and used in diverse applications. This long lag time and diffuse pathway make it legally and scientifically impossible to attribute specific health outcomes or environmental damage to a particular manufacturer or product. Without clear attribution, the tort liability system, which relies on identifying a polluter and a victim, cannot function as a corrective mechanism. The polluter cannot be held accountable for specific damages, and victims cannot seek compensation through market-based legal action. This opacity structurally prevents the internalization of welfare costs through liability. The third step in the proof introduces Axiom A3, Stockholm Convention Arbitrage. This axiom addresses the limitations of the primary international regulatory framework for persistent organic pollutants. The Stockholm Convention's process for listing and restricting chemicals is inherently slow, requiring extensive scientific review, treaty amendments, and national implementation, typically taking 5-15 years per substance. Chemical manufacturers exploit this regulatory lag through 'regrettable substitution,' reformulating existing chemicals into structurally similar analogues that are not yet listed under the Convention. This restarts the regulatory cycle, as new evidence of harm must be accumulated for each new substance. This structural arbitrage ensures that regulatory efforts will always lag behind chemical innovation, preventing a comprehensive closure of the welfare gap. The conjunction of these three axioms – A1, A2, and A3 – leads to the impossibility result. Axiom A1 guarantees that the welfare cost is intrinsically linked to the private payoff. Axiom A2 ensures that this welfare cost cannot be effectively observed or priced by market mechanisms due to attribution opacity. Axiom A3 demonstrates that the institutional capacity to close the residual gap through regulation is systematically undermined by industry's ability to circumvent the listing process. Therefore, no private bargain, no Pigouvian tax set at politically feasible levels, and no voluntary industry reform can reduce the system beta below the impossibility floor of 3.4. The welfare gap is structurally irreducible because the physical properties of the chemicals, the inherent difficulty in tracing their harm, and the limitations of the regulatory framework combine to create a self-reinforcing system of welfare destruction. The Monte Carlo simulation confirms this structural character, showing that the probability of βW falling below 1.0 is 0.0000%, and only 0.1490% of draws fall below the impossibility floor of 3.4. This means the welfare cost is thermodynamically locked in by persistence and bioaccumulation chemistry, and cannot be eliminated without fundamentally altering the production process or the institutional architecture.

welfareAnalysis.pi: $70.0B/yr. This represents the annual net surplus captured by the industry above the competitive baseline. It is not revenue, but the economic rent that would disappear under perfect competition or perfect regulation. For persistent organic pollutants, this figure is estimated from a combination of industry financial reports, regulatory filings, and academic estimates of producer surplus. This estimate is deliberately conservative; including indirect private benefits would increase private payoff and decrease system beta.
welfareAnalysis.deltaW: $435.8B/yr. This represents the total annual reduction in system welfare attributable to persistent organic pollutant production and persistence. It is aggregated across six channels: neurodevelopmental damage, metabolic and carcinogenic health burden, remediation costs, ecological contamination, Arctic and Indigenous welfare destruction, and governance failure. This figure is a flow, not a stock, measuring the ongoing welfare destruction that can be reduced or eliminated by intervention.

channels.name: Neurodevelopmental Damage from Endocrine Disruption
channels.description: This channel captures the irreversible cognitive and developmental harm caused by persistent organic pollutants, primarily polybrominated diphenyl ethers and organochlorine pesticides. These chemicals disrupt thyroid hormone signaling during fetal brain development, leading to lost IQ points and intellectual disability. The definitive calibration comes from the NYU Langone research team, whose 2015–2016 publications established 11 million lost IQ points and 43,000 cases of intellectual disability annually in the United States from polybrominated diphenyl ethers alone, monetized at 266 billion dollars per year. The European Union's earlier ban on polybrominated diphenyl ethers resulted in a significantly lower neurodevelopmental burden of 12.6 billion dollars per year, demonstrating the effectiveness of regulatory intervention. The uncertainty resides primarily in the attribution fraction, but the estimate is well-established through multiple peer-reviewed replications. This channel accounts for 40% of the total welfare cost.

channels.name: Metabolic and Carcinogenic Health Burden
channels.description: Beyond neurodevelopment, persistent organic pollutants cause metabolic disruption (obesity, type 2 diabetes, metabolic syndrome), reproductive harm (infertility, cryptorchidism), immune suppression, and cancer. Polychlorinated biphenyl exposure interferes with lipid metabolism; dioxin (2,3,7,8-Tetrachlorodibenzo-p-dioxin) is an International Agency for Research on Cancer Group 1 human carcinogen; dichlorodiphenyltrichloroethane/dichlorodiphenyldichloroethylene exposure is associated with increased risk of type 2 diabetes and reproductive dysfunction. After subtracting the neurodevelopmental component, approximately 29.3 billion dollars per year in the United States is attributable to metabolic, reproductive, and other endpoints. Agent Orange/dioxin liabilities for United States veterans amount to approximately 4–5 billion dollars per year. The European Union's metabolic and other endocrine-disrupting chemical burden is estimated at 75–100 billion dollars per year. This channel accounts for 22% of the total welfare cost.

channels.name: Site Remediation and Perpetual Management
channels.description: This channel quantifies the extraordinary costs required to clean up persistent organic pollutant-contaminated sites, including dredging, thermal treatment, sediment capping, and groundwater pump-and-treat systems. These efforts frequently fail to achieve target concentrations, leading to perpetual monitoring and containment obligations. The annual United States Superfund amortization alone is 3.7–3.8 billion dollars per year. Global total remediation amortization, including international commitments for Vietnam, Africa, and the Great Lakes, is approximately 4.5–5.5 billion dollars per year. Remediation expenditure is a mitigation cost, not a double-counting of health damages. It represents spending required to partially undo past damage and reduce future exposure. This channel accounts for 7% of the total welfare cost.

channels.name: Ecological and Food-Chain Contamination
channels.description: Persistent organic pollutants bioaccumulate and biomagnify through food webs, reaching concentrations in apex predators thousands of times higher than background levels. This damages fisheries (consumption advisories, commercial losses), agricultural productivity (livestock contamination, as in the 1999 Belgian dioxin crisis), biodiversity (raptor population collapse from dichlorodiphenyltrichloroethane egg-shell thinning), and ecosystem service provision. The Belgian dioxin crisis alone cost an estimated 465 million to 1.54 billion euros. University of Michigan estimated that Great Lakes restoration would yield 30–40 billion dollars in increased property values and commercial benefits, implying that contamination reduces ecosystem services by at least this amount. This channel accounts for 15% of the total welfare cost.

channels.name: Arctic and Indigenous Welfare Destruction
channels.description: The global distillation effect transports semi-volatile persistent organic pollutants from temperate emission sources to polar regions, where cold temperatures trap them. Once in the Arctic, persistent organic pollutants biomagnify through lipid-rich food webs, leading to concentrations in Indigenous populations up to 7 times those of southern populations. Contamination of traditional foods forces dietary transition toward expensive, imported, processed foods, driving secondary epidemics of obesity and diabetes while destroying cultural identity. This channel is monetized through healthcare costs (2–5 billion dollars per year), dietary transition costs (3–8 billion dollars per year), and cultural and environmental justice costs (3–12 billion dollars per year). Climate change is amplifying this by remobilizing legacy persistent organic pollutants from thawing permafrost. This channel accounts for 10% of the total welfare cost.

channels.name: Governance Failure and Regulatory Capture
channels.description: Chemical industry lobbying preserves market access, delays regulation, secures exemptions, and manufactures scientific doubt, extending the period of profitable persistent organic pollutant production and application. This channel is a meta-channel, amplifying damages in all other channels by postponing institutional corrections. Direct lobbying expenditure by the American Chemistry Council and European Chemical Industry Council is 110–120 million dollars per year. Product-defense science costs 50–100 million dollars per year. The indirect welfare cost of regulatory delay is estimated at 2.3–4.3 billion dollars per year, attributing a 5-year average delay across major persistent organic pollutants. The United States' failure to ratify the Stockholm Convention, driven by lobbying, has left the world's largest chemical producer operating outside the global persistent organic pollutant governance framework. This channel accounts for 6% of the total welfare cost.

welfareAnalysis.monteCarlo:
The Monte Carlo simulation is performed to construct the 90% confidence interval for the system beta and to assess the robustness of the welfare-destroying classification. The methodology involves 100,000 independent draws from the joint distribution of channel costs, aggregating them into a total welfare cost, and computing the system beta for each draw. Each channel's direct cost estimate is sampled from a triangular distribution defined by minimum, mode, and maximum values. For example, the neurodevelopmental channel's cost is sampled from a triangular distribution with a minimum of 220 billion dollars per year, a mode of 280 billion dollars per year, and a maximum of 340 billion dollars per year. Persistence multipliers for each channel are sampled from uniform distributions within ±20% of their point estimates. For instance, the neurodevelopmental channel's persistence multiplier is sampled from a uniform distribution between 0.80 and 1.20, with a point estimate of 1.0. The private payoff is sampled from a uniform distribution between 60 and 80 billion dollars per year. The five channel draws are correlated using a Cholesky decomposition of the correlation matrix, with a uniform correlation coefficient of ρ = 0.3 applied across all channel pairs. This correlation structure reflects the empirical observation that welfare channels are not independent; mortality increases typically co-occur with morbidity increases, and governance failures exacerbate other channels. The simulation uses seed = 42 for reproducibility across all System Asset Pricing Model papers. The 100,000 draws ensure precision, with the median estimate changing by less than 0.5% when the number of draws is halved. The results show a median system beta of 6.23, a mean of 6.37, and a standard deviation of 1.35. The 90% confidence interval is [4.4, 8.8]. The probability that the system beta is less than 1.0 is 0.0000%, confirming that legacy persistent organic pollutants are a severe welfare destroyer under all plausible parameterizations. The probability that the system beta is less than the impossibility floor of 3.4 is 0.1490%, confirming its structural character. The classification as welfare-destroying is invariant to all plausible parameter choices. Even if all welfare channels are reduced by 50% or the private payoff is doubled, the system beta remains well above 1.0. The Monte Carlo distribution of the system beta is robust across lognormal, normal, and triangular distribution types, with a maximum coefficient of variation across distributions of less than 5.3%. This confirms that the impossibility result does not depend on specific distributional assumptions. The Monte Carlo simulation provides a robust and transparent assessment of the welfare costs, demonstrating that the conclusions are not artifacts of central tendency but hold in the overwhelming majority of simulated draws. The simulation confirms that legacy persistent organic pollutants destroy at least 3 dollars of welfare per dollar of private payoff even under the most favorable assumptions.
